narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on factors like academic history, test scores, and background to forecast bar exam success on the first try, estimated a 41.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This prediction stems from a careful analysis of your profile as a Black male applicant, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 38, part-time enrollment plans, high family income bracket, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1.00 each—both well below the typical student's decile around 5 or 6. The two biggest factors pushing the model toward predicting failure were your Year 3 decile ranking and your Year 1 decile ranking; these low placements signal to the model weaker early and later academic performance relative to peers, strongly influencing the overall risk assessment despite other positives. On a brighter note, your LSAT score stood out as the most significant factor pulling the prediction toward success, as it's slightly above the average of 36.7 and shows solid standardized testing ability. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who often have stronger decile rankings and full-time enrollment (92.7% are full-time versus your part-time status)—your profile appeared riskier in those key academic consistency areas, tipping the balance. While your high family income is a supportive element, it wasn't enough to offset the primary concerns. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven call to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-baccalaureate program to improve those decile equivalents. You might also explore full-time options if feasible, as they align better with successful profiles. Many applicants in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these gaps—reach out to admissions for targeted advice or consider schools with holistic reviews that weigh your unique story more heavily. With focused improvements, you can reshape this narrative for future applications.
